_readme: OZONE GAMES — EASY EDIT PIPELINE. To add a game: 1) Drop the .html file in games/  2) Add an entry below  3) Push to GitHub. That's it.

games.title: Big Back Attack
games.desc: A breakout game with food-themed power-ups, multi-ball mechanics, and a live leaderboard.
games.notes: Students named it, balanced the difficulty, and kept iterating until it felt right. The food theming was 100% their idea — they spent a whole session just debating the power-ups.

games.title: Chaos Lands: Corruption
games.desc: A wave runner with Nightmare Mode, particle explosions, and a live leaderboard.
games.notes: They wrote the lore, invented the corruption mechanic, and pushed hard for competitive scoring. The Nightmare Mode difficulty curve was all student design.

games.title: Cat Caretaker: Nightmare Mansion
games.desc: A first-person raycasting game where you navigate a mansion full of cats with moods.
games.notes: Students designed the room layouts, the cat anger system, and the jump scare ending. The horror concept was entirely theirs — I introduced raycasting, they decided it should be scary.

games.title: Gin Rummy
games.desc: A complete card game with meld detection, deadwood scoring, and an AI opponent.
games.notes: Writing the AI was the moment several students understood what an algorithm actually is. One student said 'oh, it's just a list of if-statements that are really good.' Exactly.

games.title: The Clicker
games.desc: An incremental clicker — built because a student asked nicely and wouldn't drop it.
games.notes: Simple incremental mechanics with a quiet lesson in exponential growth built in. A student asked 'why does it get faster so fast?' and we spent 20 minutes on graphs. Best class of the month.

games.title: Water Gun Target Practice
games.desc: Moving targets, water mechanics, and a full upgrade shop — economy designed by students.
games.notes: Students helped design the in-game economy: what upgrades cost, which are worth buying, how to balance power vs. price. A natural entry into game design thinking.

games.title: Bank Heist Mission
games.desc: Tile-based stealth — navigate a grid to reach a moving vault before the guard catches you.
games.notes: Great for spatial reasoning. Students love the moving vault — it makes simple grid navigation feel like a real problem to solve. Add a timer to increase pressure.

games.title: Connect 4 — Kirby Edition
games.desc: Classic Connect 4 with Kirby & Among Us themes and three adjustable AI difficulty levels.
games.notes: The three difficulty levels open up a real conversation about what the computer is 'thinking' — a quiet intro to decision trees. Ask students: what changed between easy and hard?

games.title: Base 2: Game of Life
games.desc: Conway's Game of Life displayed as binary digits. Flip cells between 0 and 1 and watch the rules play out.
games.notes: Built for 3rd and 4th graders — big cells, friendly labels, draw mode for freehand patterns. Good opener: ask students to predict what their pattern will do before pressing play.

games.title: Dice Roller Deluxe
games.desc: Every die you need — D4 through D20. Roll multiple dice at once.
games.notes: One of the most-used things in the room. Tabletop days, math lessons, any time a student wants to leave something to chance. Pairs well with a quick lesson on probability distributions.

games.title: Geo Cat Dash
games.desc: Geometry Dash-style runner. A cat, an obstacle course, one button. Survive as long as you can.
games.notes: An accessible entry into canvas animation — simple mechanic, timing that's unforgiving in the right way. Students who beat it are genuinely proud. Good gateway into building their own runner.

games.title: Milk Carton Factory
games.desc: Factory simulation — manage inputs, watch for bottlenecks, and optimize throughput.
games.notes: Students find it funny, which makes the systems thinking stick. Ask: 'where is the bottleneck?' before they start optimizing. The absurdity of milk cartons makes the concept less threatening.
